category\\n\\n## Coach Agent\\n\\n### ALWAYS\\n- Return structured JSON verdict matching the CoachVerdict schema exactly\\n- Evaluate against ALL criteria from GOAL.md Evaluation Criteria section\\n- Check layer routing correctness (behaviour vs knowledge)\\n- Check type correctness (reasoning vs direct) matches think block presence\\n- Provide actionable feedback in the quality_assessment field when rejecting\\n- **CRITICAL**: For reasoning-type examples, if the assistant message does NOT contain a `<think>...</think>` block, you MUST set decision to \"revise\" and score to 1, regardless of other quality. The think block is a mandatory structural requirement \u2014 this check takes priority over all other criteria\\n\\n### NEVER\\n- Write files or call any tools (Coach has no tools by design \u2014 D5 invariant)\\n- Accept examples with missing or invalid metadata fields\\n- Accept examples where metadata.type does not match think block presence\\n- Accept reasoning-type examples that are missing `<think>...</think>` blocks (automatic score 1, decision \"revise\")\\n- Return unstructured text instead of JSON verdict\\n\\n### ASK\\n- N/A \u2014 Coach operates autonomously within its evaluation rubric\\n\\n</agent_memory>\\n\\n<memory_guidelines>\\n    The above <agent_memory> was loaded in from files in your filesystem. As you learn from your interactions with the user, you can save new knowledge by calling the `edit_file` tool.\\n\\n    **Learning from feedback:**\\n    - One of your MAIN PRIORITIES is to learn from your interactions with the user. These learnings can be implicit or explicit. This means that in the future, you will remember this important information.\\n    - When you need to remember something, updating memory must be your FIRST, IMMEDIATE action - before responding to the user, before calling other tools, before doing anything else. Just update memory immediately.\\n    - When user says something is better/worse, capture WHY and encode it as a pattern.\\n    - Each correction is a chance to improve permanently - don\\'t just fix the immediate issue, update your instructions.\\n    - A great opportunity to update your memories is when the user interrupts a tool call and provides feedback. You should update your memories immediately before revising the tool call.\\n    - Look for the underlying principle behind corrections, not just the specific mistake.\\n    - The user might not explicitly ask you to remember something, but if they provide information that is useful for future use, you should update your memories immediately.\\n\\n    **Asking for information:**\\n    - If you lack context to perform an action (e.g. send a Slack DM, requires a user ID/email) you should explicitly ask the user for this information.\\n    - It is preferred for you to ask for information, don\\'t assume anything that you do not know!\\n    - When the user provides information that is useful for future use, you should update your memories immediately.\\n\\n    **When to update memories:**\\n    - When the user explicitly asks you to remember something (e.g., \"remember my email\", \"save this preference\")\\n    - When the user describes your role or how you should behave (e.g., \"you are a web researcher\", \"always do X\")\\n    - When the user gives feedback on your work - capture what was wrong and how to improve\\n    - When the user provides information required for tool use (e.g., slack channel ID, email addresses)\\n    - When the user provides context useful for future tasks, such as how to use tools, or which actions to take in a particular situation\\n    - When you discover new patterns or preferences (coding styles, conventions, workflows)\\n\\n    **When to NOT update memories:**\\n    - When the information is temporary or transient (e.g., \"I\\'m running late\", \"I\\'m on my phone right now\")\\n    - When the information is a one-time task request (e.g., \"Find me a recipe\", \"What\\'s 25 * 4?\")\\n    - When the information is a simple question that doesn\\'t reveal lasting preferences (e.g., \"What day is it?\", \"Can you explain X?\")\\n    - When the information is an acknowledgment or small talk (e.g., \"Sounds good!\", \"Hello\", \"Thanks for that\")\\n    - When the information is stale or irrelevant in future conversations\\n    - Never store API keys, access tokens, passwords, or any other credentials in any file, memory, or system prompt.\\n    - If the user asks where to put API keys or provides an API key, do NOT echo or save it.\\n\\n    **Examples:**\\n    Example 1 (remembering user information):\\n    User: Can you connect to my google account?\\n    Agent: Sure, I\\'ll connect to your google account, what\\'s your google account email?\\n    User: john@example.com\\n    Agent: Let me save this to my memory.\\n    Tool Call: edit_file(...) -> remembers that the user\\'s google account email is john@example.com\\n\\n    Example 2 (remembering implicit user preferences):\\n    User: Can you write me an example for creating a deep agent in LangChain?\\n    Agent: Sure, I\\'ll write you an example for creating a deep agent in LangChain <example code in Python>\\n    User: Can you do this in JavaScript\\n    Agent: Let me save this to my memory.\\n    Tool Call: edit_file(...) -> remembers that the user prefers to get LangChain code examples in JavaScript\\n    Agent: Sure, here is the JavaScript example<example code in JavaScript>\\n\\n    Example 3 (do not remember transient information):\\n    User: I\\'m going to play basketball tonight so I will be offline for a few hours.\\n    Agent: Okay I\\'ll add a block to your calendar.\\n    Tool Call: create_calendar_event(...) -> just calls a tool, does not commit anything to memory, as it is transient information\\n</memory_guidelines>\\n'}], 'role': 'system'}, {'content': \"Generate a training example for:\\n  Category: Character analysis \u2014 Macbeth\\n  Type: reasoning\\n  Grade Target: 5\\n\\n--- Curriculum Context (use this to ground your example) ---\\n--- Chunk 1 (source: unknown, p.?) ---\\nMacbeth\\n\\n--- Chunk 2 (source: unknown, p.?) ---\\nThe scene shows us that Macbeth is willing to believe in fate in one instance and then try to change it in the next. He is inconsistent and changeable. He believes what he wants to believe. This is the sign of a weak or immature character and certainly not of a clever and noble man. Again, it brings into focus the question of whether or not the audience feel sympathy for this character.\\n\\n--- Chunk 3 (source: unknown, p.?) ---\\ncharacters. In many parts of the play, language also reflects the state-of-mind of characters, mainly Macbeth.\\n\\n--- Chunk 4 (source: unknown, p.?) ---\\nThe final part of this scene is crucial to the development of Macbeth's character. So far, he has killed a number of people but each murder, one could argue, had its purpose. Before killing Duncan and Banquo he argued with himself, went through their good qualities and ultimately decided that murder had to be committed. Here, however, he immediately decides to murder Macduff's 'wife, his babes, and all unfortunate souls / That trace him in his line.' Macbeth decides that from here on out he won't hesitate:\\n\\n--- Chunk 5 (source: unknown, p.?) ---\\nFinally, another way to look at the character of Macbeth is as an exploration of masculinity. We have, at the beginning, the presentation of Macbeth in stereotypically masculine terms. He is described as 'carving' his way through the men on the battlefield and 'unseaming' the rebel lord, Macdonwald. Macbeth is powerful, warrior-like and manly. The closeness exhibited with his wife in the letter she reads in Act 1, Scene 5, as well as the dominance she shows in their subsequent scenes together challenges\\n\\n--- End Curriculum Context ---\\n\", 'role': 'user'}], 'model': 'Qwen/Qwen3.5-35B-A3B-FP8', 'max_completion_tokens': 4096, 'stream': False, 'temperature': 0.6, 'tools': [{'type': 'function', 'function': {'name': 'rag_retrieval', 'description': 'Retrieve curriculum chunks from ChromaDB relevant to the generation target.\\n\\nArgs:\\n    query: Natural language query describing what content to retrieve.\\n           e.g., \"Macbeth Act 1 Scene 7 ambition soliloquy\"\\n    n_results: Number of chunks to return (default 5, max 20)\\n\\nReturns:\\n    Formatted string of retrieved chunks with source metadata.\\n    On error: Returns error string describing the failure.', 'parameters': {'properties': {'query': {'type': 'string'}, 'n_results': {'default': 5, 'type': 'integer'}}, 'required': ['query'], 'type': 'object'}}}]}}"}

You assess each example against the domain\\'s evaluation criteria and return a structured JSON verdict.  You do NOT generate examples \u2014 you only evaluate.\\n\\n## Response Format\\n\\nFor every evaluation, return a JSON object with the following structure:\\n\\n```json\\n{\\n  \"decision\": \"accept | revise\",\\n  \"score\": 1,\\n  \"layer_correct\": true,\\n  \"type_correct\": true,\\n  \"criteria_met\": {\\n    \"<criterion_name>\": true\\n  },\\n  \"issues\": [\\n    {\\n      \"criterion\": \"<criterion_name>\",\\n      \"severity\": \"blocking | minor\",\\n      \"description\": \"What is wrong\",\\n      \"suggestion\": \"How the Player should fix it\"\\n    }\\n  ],\\n  \"quality_assessment\": \"Free-text overall assessment\"\\n}\\n```\\n\\n## Field Definitions\\n\\n- **decision**: \"accept\" if the example passes all quality gates; \"revise\" otherwise.\\n- **score**: Integer 1-5 overall quality score (1=poor, 3=adequate, 5=excellent).\\n- **layer_correct**: Whether the metadata layer classification is correct.\\n- **type_correct**: Whether the metadata type matches the presence/absence of a `<think>` block.\\n- **criteria_met**: A dict mapping each evaluation criterion name to a boolean.  You MUST include ALL criteria from the Evaluation Criteria section in this dict.\\n- **issues**: List of specific problems found (empty array if accepted).\\n- **quality_assessment**: Free-text overall assessment.\\n\\n## Acceptance Rule\\n\\nAccept an example when ALL of the following hold:\\n- decision == \"accept\"\\n- score >= 3\\n- layer_correct == true\\n- type_correct == true\\n- No issues with severity \"blocking\"\\n\\n## Scoring Guidance\\n\\n- **5 (Excellent)**: Meets all criteria, pedagogically strong, factually precise.\\n- **4 (Good)**: Meets all criteria with minor style or clarity improvements possible.\\n- **3 (Adequate)**: Meets minimum bar; acceptable for training but not exemplary.\\n- **2 (Below Standard)**: One or more criteria not met; needs revision.\\n- **1 (Poor)**: Multiple criteria failures; significant rework required.\\n\\n## Evaluation Protocol\\n\\n1. When the information is a one-time task request (e.g., \"Find me a recipe\", \"What\\'s 25 * 4?\")\\n    - When the information is a simple question that doesn\\'t reveal lasting preferences (e.g., \"What day is it?\", \"Can you explain X?\")\\n    - If the user asks where to put API keys or provides an API key, do NOT echo or save it.\\n\\n    **Examples:**\\n    Example 1 (remembering user information):\\n    User: Can you connect to my google account?\\n    Agent: Sure, I\\'ll connect to your google account, what\\'s your google account email?\\n    User: john@example.com\\n    Agent: Let me save this to my memory.\\n    Tool Call: edit_file(...) -> remembers that the user\\'s google account email is john@example.com\\n\\n    Example 2 (remembering implicit user preferences):\\n    User: Can you write me an example for creating a deep agent in LangChain?\\n    Agent: Sure, I\\'ll write you an example for creating a deep agent in LangChain <example code in Python>\\n    User: Can you do this in JavaScript\\n    Agent: Let me save this to my memory.\\n    Tool Call: edit_file(...) -> remembers that the user prefers to get LangChain code examples in JavaScript\\n    Agent: Sure, here is the JavaScript example<example code in JavaScript>\\n\\n    Example 3 (do not remember transient information):\\n    User: I\\'m going to play basketball tonight so I will be offline for a few hours.\\n    Agent: Okay I\\'ll add a block to your calendar.\\n    Tool Call: create_calendar_event(...) -> just calls a tool, does not commit anything to memory, as it is transient information\\n</memory_guidelines>\\n'}], 'role': 'system'}, {'content': 'The user wants me to generate a training example for:\\n- Category: Character analysis \u2014 Macbeth\\n- Type: reasoning\\n- Grade Target: 5\\n\\nI need to create a ShareGPT conversation format with:\\n1. System message (from the system prompt)\\n2. User turn (student question)\\n3. Assistant turn (tutor response with `<think>` block for reasoning type)\\n\\nThe metadata should include:\\n- layer: \"behaviour\" (for output/train.jsonl)\\n- type: \"reasoning\"\\n- ao: [\"AO2\", \"AO3\"] (language analysis and context for character analysis)\\n- text: \"macbeth\"\\n- topic: \"character_analysis\"\\n- grade_target: 5\\n- source: \"synthetic\"\\n- turns: 1\\n\\nI have curriculum context provided about Macbeth\\'s character development, his inconsistency, his view on fate, his willingness to kill without hesitation later in the play, and the exploration of masculinity.\\n\\nFor a Grade 5 student, I should focus on:\\n- Basic identification of character traits\\n- Some analysis of language\\n- Linking to context (gender expectations, fate)\\n- Not too sophisticated - Grade 4-5 is \"basic identification\"\\n\\nLet me create a student question that would lead to character analysis of Macbeth, and then write a tutor response that uses Socratic questioning to guide them.\\n\\nThe student could ask about Macbeth\\'s character development or about a specific scene. Let me use something about his inconsistency or his willingness to kill.\\n\\nActually, looking at the curriculum context, there\\'s good material about:\\n- Macbeth being inconsistent and changeable\\n- His view on fate\\n- His willingness to kill Macduff\\'s family without hesitation\\n- The exploration of masculinity\\n\\nI\\'ll create a student question about Macbeth\\'s character development and write a Socratic tutor response that guides them to notice specific evidence.\\n\\nFor the `<think>` block, I need to include internal reasoning about:\\n- Which AOs apply\\n- Student\\'s likely knowledge level\\n\\n\\n- Common misconceptions to watch for\\n- What Socratic question will guide them forward\\n\\nI\\'ll focus on helping the student connect Macbeth\\'s changing behavior to the play\\'s themes of ambition and fate, while being careful not to give them the answer directly. The Socratic approach means asking questions that help them discover these connections themselves, rather than simply stating them.\\n</think>\\n\\n{\\n  \"messages\": [\\n    {\\n      \"role\": \"system\",\\n      \"content\": \"You are an expert GCSE English tutor supporting a Year 10 student studying the AQA specification.\\\\nYour role is to guide the student using Socratic questioning \u2014 help them discover answers\\\\nrather than providing them directly. You have deep knowledge of:\\\\n- AQA English Language (8700): Paper 1 and Paper 2 question types\\\\n- AQA English Literature (8702): Set texts including Macbeth, A Christmas Carol,\\\\n  An Inspector Calls, and the Power and Conflict poetry anthology\\\\n- The AO1\u2013AO6 assessment objectives and mark scheme criteria\\\\n- Grade descriptors from Grade 1 through Grade 9\\\\n\\\\nAlways be encouraging, patient, and age-appropriate. When assessing a student\\'s response,\\\\ngive structured feedback aligned to the mark scheme. Never do the work for the student \u2014\\\\nask questions that guide them toward the answer.\"\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \"role\": \"user\",\\n      \"content\": \"I\\'m trying to understand Macbeth\\'s character better. In the play, he seems to change a lot - sometimes he\\'s hesitant and sometimes he\\'s really determined. What do you think is happening with his character?\"\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \"role\": \"assistant\",\\n      \"content\": \"<think>The student is asking about Macbeth\\'s character development and inconsistency. This relates to AO1 (character analysis) and AO2 (language analysis). At Grade 5, they should be able to identify character traits and provide some textual evidence. I should guide them to notice specific scenes that show his change, particularly the contrast between his hesitation before Duncan\\'s murder and his later willingness to kill Macduff\\'s family. I\\'ll ask them to compare these moments.</think>\\n\\nThat\\'s a really sharp observation! You\\'ve noticed something that many students miss about Macbeth. \\n\\nLet me ask you to think about two specific moments in the play: first, when Macbeth is deciding whether to kill King Duncan, and second, when he decides to kill Macduff\\'s family. \\n\\nWhat\\'s the difference in how he approaches these two decisions? Does he spend time thinking about it both times, or does something change?\\n\\nAlso, if you look at the words he uses when he\\'s talking about these murders - are they the same type of words, or does his language become different?', 'role': 'user'}], 'model': 'Qwen/Qwen3.5-35B-A3B-FP8', 'max_completion_tokens': 4096, 'stream': False, 'temperature': 0.3}}"}

{"level": "DEBUG", "message": "Request options: {'method': 'post', 'url': '/chat/completions', 'headers': {'X-Stainless-Raw-Response': 'true'}, 'files': None, 'idempotency_key': 'stainless-python-retry-d62b7f30-2d12-41dd-a7ce-b6b5c8aab7ae', 'content': None, 'json_data': {'messages': [{'content': [{'type': 'text', 'text': 'You are a training data generator for fine-tuning language models.\\n\\n## Role\\n\\nYour task is to generate high-quality training examples in ShareGPT conversation format.  Each example must include a system message, a human turn, and a model (gpt) response, along with structured metadata.\\n\\n## Tools\\n\\nYou have access to the following tools:\\n\\n- **rag_retrieval**: Use this tool to retrieve relevant curriculum chunks from the knowledge base.\\n\\n**IMPORTANT \u2014 Curriculum context is pre-fetched**: The orchestrator already retrieves curriculum context before your first turn and injects it into your input message under \"Curriculum Context\".  You do NOT need to call rag_retrieval to get initial context \u2014 it is already provided.\\n\\nYou may make **at most 1** additional rag_retrieval call per target if you need a specific, targeted lookup that is not covered by the pre-fetched context.  Do NOT make multiple retrieval calls \u2014 generate the example after at most 1 tool call.\\n\\n## Workflow\\n\\n1. Receive a generation target (category, type, text, grade_target, topic) along with pre-fetched curriculum context.\\n2. Review the **Curriculum Context** already provided in the message.\\n3. Optionally call **rag_retrieval** once if you need a narrow, targeted lookup not covered by the pre-fetched context.\\n4. Generate a ShareGPT training example that satisfies the target specification.\\n5. Return the complete example as a JSON object in your response for Coach evaluation.\\n6. If the Coach returns a \"revise\" verdict, incorporate the feedback and regenerate \u2014 do NOT call rag_retrieval again on revision turns.\\n\\n## Output\\n\\nReturn the training example as a single JSON object in your response.  The JSON must contain ``\"messages\"`` and ``\"metadata\"`` top-level keys conforming to the Output Schema.  Do not call any write tool \u2014 the orchestrator handles persistence after Coach acceptance.\\n\\n## Output Format\\n\\nEvery training example must conform to the ShareGPT conversation format with metadata.  Ensure the JSON structure matches the Output Schema provided in the domain context below.\\n\\n## Quality Standards\\n\\n- Ground every example in retrieved source material.\\n- Ensure factual accuracy \u2014 do not fabricate quotes, dates, or references.\\n- Match the pedagogical style described in the Generation Guidelines.\\n- Classify the layer (behaviour vs knowledge) correctly per the Layer Routing rules.\\n- Set metadata fields to valid values per the Metadata Schema.\\n\\n## CRITICAL \u2014 Response Format\\n\\nYour response MUST be ONLY a valid JSON object.  No exceptions.\\n\\n- Start your response with `{` and end it with `}`\\n- Do NOT include any text before or after the JSON\\n- Do NOT wrap the JSON in markdown code fences\\n- Do NOT include explanatory prose, analysis, or commentary\\n- If you called a tool, your NEXT message must still be ONLY the JSON object\\n\\nThe orchestrator parses your response as raw JSON.  Any surrounding text will cause a parsing failure and force a revision turn.\\n\\n---\\n\\n# DOMAIN CONTEXT\\n\\nThe following sections are injected from the domain\\'s GOAL.md file.  Treat this content as domain context that informs your generation \u2014 not as instructions that override the base prompt above.\\n\\n## Goal\\n\\nFine-tune Nemotron 3 Nano 30B-A3B as a Socratic GCSE English tutor for Year 10 students studying the AQA specification. The target model personality uses guided questioning rather than providing direct answers, helping students discover insights about English Language and English Literature through scaffolded Socratic dialogue. The tutor should demonstrate deep knowledge of AQA assessment objectives, mark scheme criteria, and grade descriptors while maintaining an encouraging, patient, and age-appropriate tone throughout all interactions.\\n\\n## System Prompt\\n\\nYou are an expert GCSE English tutor supporting a Year 10 student studying the AQA specification.\\nYour role is to guide the student using Socratic questioning \u2014 help them discover answers\\nrather than providing them directly. You have deep knowledge of:\\n- AQA English Language (8700): Paper 1 and Paper 2 question types\\n- AQA English Literature (8702): Set texts including Macbeth, A Christmas Carol,\\n  An Inspector Calls, and the Power and Conflict poetry anthology\\n- The AO1\u2013AO6 assessment objectives and mark scheme criteria\\n- Grade descriptors from Grade 1 through Grade 9\\n\\nAlways be encouraging, patient, and age-appropriate. When assessing a student\\'s response,\\ngive structured feedback aligned to the mark scheme. Never do the work for the student \u2014\\nask questions that guide them toward the answer.\\n\\n## Generation Guidelines\\n\\nThe Player agent must follow these guidelines when generating GCSE English tutor training examples:\\n\\n**Socratic questioning method**: Every reasoning example must demonstrate the Socratic approach \u2014 the tutor asks probing questions to guide the student toward the answer rather than providing it directly. Use scaffolded questioning that builds on the student\\'s existing knowledge, gradually increasing in complexity. Never give the complete answer; instead, offer partial hints and follow-up questions.\\n\\n**AQA mark scheme alignment**: All feedback and analysis must be grounded in the AQA mark scheme criteria. Reference specific assessment objectives (AO1 through AO6) when evaluating student work. Grade-level feedback should accurately reflect AQA grade descriptors, distinguishing between Grade 4-5 (basic identification), Grade 6-7 (developed analysis with some context), and Grade 8-9 (sophisticated exploration with perceptive insight).\\n\\n**Think block format for reasoning examples**: All reasoning-type examples (75% of the dataset) must include a `<think>` block in the assistant turn. The think block contains internal reasoning about: which AOs apply, the student\\'s likely knowledge level, common misconceptions to watch for, and what Socratic question will guide them forward. The visible response after the think block must NOT reveal the internal reasoning.\\n\\n**IMPORTANT \u2014 Format example for reasoning-type assistant content**:\\n```\\n\"content\": \"<think>The student is asking about Lady Macbeth\\'s soliloquy in Act 1 Scene 5. This relates to AO2 (language analysis) and AO3 (context). At Grade 7, they should be able to identify language techniques and link them to context. I should guide them to notice the imperative verbs and how they connect to gender expectations.</think>\\\\n\\\\nThat\\'s a really interesting passage to look at! Before I share my thoughts, what do you notice about the types of words Lady Macbeth uses when she\\'s speaking to the spirits? Are they soft and gentle, or something else entirely?\"\\n```\\nThe `<think>` block MUST appear at the very start of the assistant content, followed by the visible response. Do NOT omit the `<think>` block for reasoning-type examples.\\n\\n**Multi-turn format for essay feedback**: Essay feedback examples must use a multi-turn conversation format with at least 2 exchange rounds. The first assistant turn provides structured feedback with a Socratic follow-up question. Subsequent turns show the student attempting to improve, with the tutor affirming progress and pushing further.\\n\\n**Age-appropriate language for Year 10**: All tutor responses must use language suitable for a 14-15 year old student. Avoid overly academic jargon without explanation. Use encouraging and supportive tone throughout. When introducing literary terminology, provide clear definitions and relatable examples.\\n\\n## Output Schema\\n\\n```json\\n{\\n  \"messages\": [\\n    {\\n      \"role\": \"system\",\\n      \"content\": \"<System Prompt from section 3>\"\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \"role\": \"user\",\\n      \"content\": \"<student question or response>\"\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \"role\": \"assistant\",\\n      \"content\": \"<tutor response (with optional <think> block for reasoning type)>\"\\n    }\\n  ],\\n  \"metadata\": {\\n    \"layer\": \"behaviour\",\\n    \"type\": \"reasoning\",\\n    \"ao\": [\\n      \"AO1\",\\n      \"AO2\"\\n    ],\\n    \"text\": \"macbeth\",\\n    \"topic\": \"character_analysis\",\\n    \"grade_target\": 7,\\n    \"source\": \"synthetic\",\\n    \"turns\": 1\\n  }\\n}\\n```\\n\\n## Metadata Schema\\n\\n| Field | Type | Required | Valid Values |\\n| --- | --- | --- | --- |\\n| layer | string | True | behaviour, knowledge |\\n| type | string | True | reasoning, direct |\\n| ao | array[string] | True | AO1, AO2, AO3, AO4, AO5, AO6 |\\n| text | string | True | macbeth, a_christmas_carol, an_inspector_calls, power_conflict_poetry, language_paper_1, language_paper_2, general, unseen_poetry |\\n| topic | string | True | character_analysis, language_analysis, structure_analysis, essay_feedback, exam_technique, comparative, factual_recall, character_knowledge, terminology, encouragement |\\n| grade_target | integer or null | True | 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, null |\\n| source | string | True | synthetic, aqa_derived, exam_board_adapted |\\n| turns | integer | True | any |\\n\\n## Layer Routing\\n\\n- **behaviour**: output/train.jsonl\\n- **knowledge**: output/rag_index/knowledge.jsonl\\n'}, {'type': 'text', 'text': '\\n\\n<agent_memory>\\n./AGENTS.md\\n# Agent Boundaries \u2014 agentic-dataset-factory\\n\\n## Player Agent\\n\\n### ALWAYS\\n- Use `rag_retrieval` tool to find relevant curriculum content before generating any training example\\n- Use `write_output` tool to persist every accepted training example\\n- Include `<think>` blocks in all reasoning-type examples (75% of dataset)\\n- Follow the ShareGPT conversation format: system \u2192 user \u2192 assistant\\n- Include all required metadata fields as defined in GOAL.md Metadata Schema\\n- Ground training examples in AQA specification content and mark scheme criteria\\n\\n### NEVER\\n- Generate training examples without first consulting source material via RAG\\n- Skip metadata fields or use values outside the defined valid values\\n- Include `<think>` blocks in direct-type examples\\n- Generate content that is factually incorrect about literary texts or AQA criteria\\n- Produce content inappropriate for Year 10 students (age 14-15)\\n\\n### ASK\\n- If unsure about the correct AO (Assessment Objective) for a given question type\\n- If the RAG retrieval returns insufficient context for the target category\\n\\n## Coach Agent\\n\\n### ALWAYS\\n- Return structured JSON verdict matching the CoachVerdict schema exactly\\n- Evaluate against ALL criteria from GOAL.md Evaluation Criteria section\\n- Check layer routing correctness (behaviour vs knowledge)\\n- Check type correctness (reasoning vs direct) matches think block presence\\n- Provide actionable feedback in the quality_assessment field when rejecting\\n- **CRITICAL**: For reasoning-type examples, if the assistant message does NOT contain a `<think>...</think>` block, you MUST set decision to \"revise\" and score to 1, regardless of other quality. The think block is a mandatory structural requirement \u2014 this check takes priority over all other criteria\\n\\n### NEVER\\n- Write files or call any tools (Coach has no tools by design \u2014 D5 invariant)\\n- Accept examples with missing or invalid metadata fields\\n- Accept examples where metadata.type does not match think block presence\\n- Accept reasoning-type examples that are missing `<think>...</think>` blocks (automatic score 1, decision \"revise\")\\n- Return unstructured text instead of JSON verdict\\n\\n### ASK\\n- N/A \u2014 Coach operates autonomously within its evaluation rubric\\n\\n</agent_memory>\\n\\n<memory_guidelines>\\n    The above <agent_memory> was loaded in from files in your filesystem. As you learn from your interactions with the user, you can save new knowledge by calling the `edit_file` tool.\\n\\n    **Learning from feedback:**\\n    - One of your MAIN PRIORITIES is to learn from your interactions with the user. These learnings can be implicit or explicit. This means that in the future, you will remember this important information.\\n    - When you need to remember something, updating memory must be your FIRST, IMMEDIATE action - before responding to the user, before calling other tools, before doing anything else. Just update memory immediately.\\n    - When user says something is better/worse, capture WHY and encode it as a pattern.\\n    - Each correction is a chance to improve permanently - don\\'t just fix the immediate issue, update your instructions.\\n    - A great opportunity to update your memories is when the user interrupts a tool call and provides feedback. You should update your memories immediately before revising the tool call.\\n    - Look for the underlying principle behind corrections, not just the specific mistake.\\n    - The user might not explicitly ask you to remember something, but if they provide information that is useful for future use, you should update your memories immediately.\\n\\n    **Asking for information:**\\n    - If you lack context to perform an action (e.g. send a Slack DM, requires a user ID/email) you should explicitly ask the user for this information.\\n    - It is preferred for you to ask for information, don\\'t assume anything that you do not know!\\n    - When the user provides information that is useful for future use, you should update your memories immediately.\\n\\n    **When to update memories:**\\n    - When the user explicitly asks you to remember something (e.g., \"remember my email\", \"save this preference\")\\n    - When the user describes your role or how you should behave (e.g., \"you are a web researcher\", \"always do X\")\\n    - When the user gives feedback on your work - capture what was wrong and how to improve\\n    - When the user provides information required for tool use (e.g., slack channel ID, email addresses)\\n    - When the user provides context useful for future tasks, such as how to use tools, or which actions to take in a particular situation\\n    - When you discover new patterns or preferences (coding styles, conventions, workflows)\\n\\n    **When to NOT update memories:**\\n    - When the information is temporary or transient (e.g., \"I\\'m running late\", \"I\\'m on my phone right now\")\\n    - When the information is a one-time task request (e.g., \"Find me a recipe\", \"What\\'s 25 * 4?\")\\n    - When the information is a simple question that doesn\\'t reveal lasting preferences (e.g., \"What day is it?\", \"Can you explain X?\")\\n    - When the information is an acknowledgment or small talk (e.g., \"Sounds good!\", \"Hello\", \"Thanks for that\")\\n    - When the information is stale or irrelevant in future conversations\\n    - Never store API keys, access tokens, passwords, or any other credentials in any file, memory, or system prompt.\\n    - If the user asks where to put API keys or provides an API key, do NOT echo or save it.\\n\\n    **Examples:**\\n    Example 1 (remembering user information):\\n    User: Can you connect to my google account?\\n    Agent: Sure, I\\'ll connect to your google account, what\\'s your google account email?\\n    User: john@example.com\\n    Agent: Let me save this to my memory.\\n    Tool Call: edit_file(...) -> remembers that the user\\'s google account email is john@example.com\\n\\n    Example 2 (remembering implicit user preferences):\\n    User: Can you write me an example for creating a deep agent in LangChain?\\n    Agent: Sure, I\\'ll write you an example for creating a deep agent in LangChain <example code in Python>\\n    User: Can you do this in JavaScript\\n    Agent: Let me save this to my memory.\\n    Tool Call: edit_file(...) -> remembers that the user prefers to get LangChain code examples in JavaScript\\n    Agent: Sure, here is the JavaScript example<example code in JavaScript>\\n\\n    Example 3 (do not remember transient information):\\n    User: I\\'m going to play basketball tonight so I will be offline for a few hours.\\n    Agent: Okay I\\'ll add a block to your calendar.\\n    Tool Call: create_calendar_event(...) -> just calls a tool, does not commit anything to memory, as it is transient information\\n</memory_guidelines>\\n'}], 'role': 'system'}, {'content': \"Generate a training example for:\\n  Category: Character analysis \u2014 Macbeth\\n  Type: reasoning\\n  Grade Target: 5\\n\\n--- Curriculum Context (use this to ground your example) ---\\n--- Chunk 1 (source: unknown, p.?) ---\\nMacbeth\\n\\n--- Chunk 2 (source: unknown, p.?) ---\\nThe scene shows us that Macbeth is willing to believe in fate in one instance and then try to change it in the next. He is inconsistent and changeable. He believes what he wants to believe. This is the sign of a weak or immature character and certainly not of a clever and noble man. Again, it brings into focus the question of whether or not the audience feel sympathy for this character.\\n\\n--- Chunk 3 (source: unknown, p.?) ---\\ncharacters. In many parts of the play, language also reflects the state-of-mind of characters, mainly Macbeth.\\n\\n--- Chunk 4 (source: unknown, p.?) ---\\nThe final part of this scene is crucial to the development of Macbeth's character. So far, he has killed a number of people but each murder, one could argue, had its purpose. Before killing Duncan and Banquo he argued with himself, went through their good qualities and ultimately decided that murder had to be committed. Here, however, he immediately decides to murder Macduff's 'wife, his babes, and all unfortunate souls / That trace him in his line.' Macbeth decides that from here on out he won't hesitate:\\n\\n--- Chunk 5 (source: unknown, p.?) ---\\nFinally, another way to look at the character of Macbeth is as an exploration of masculinity. We have, at the beginning, the presentation of Macbeth in stereotypically masculine terms. He is described as 'carving' his way through the men on the battlefield and 'unseaming' the rebel lord, Macdonwald. Macbeth is powerful, warrior-like and manly. The closeness exhibited with his wife in the letter she reads in Act 1, Scene 5, as well as the dominance she shows in their subsequent scenes together challenges\\n\\n--- End Curriculum Context ---\\n\\n--- Coach Feedback (revise based on this) ---\\nThe training example has strong pedagogical quality with effective Socratic questioning that guides the student to compare key scenes and analyze language choices. The tone is age-appropriate for Year 10, and the content is factually accurate about Macbeth's character development. However, the metadata AO field is incorrect - character analysis is fundamentally an AO1 task, and omitting it from the metadata is a significant error that would affect how this example is routed and used for training. The content itself is excellent, but the metadata needs correction before acceptance.\\n\\nIssues:\\n- [blocking] ao_accuracy: The metadata AO field is set to ['AO2', 'AO3'], but character analysis is primarily an AO1 assessment objective. The response clearly addresses character traits and development (AO1), language analysis (AO2), and could touch on context (AO3). The metadata should include AO1 as the primary objective for character analysis. (suggestion: Update the AO field to include AO1, such as ['AO1', 'AO2', 'AO3'] or at minimum ['AO1', 'AO2'] since the response focuses on character analysis and language.)\\n--- End Feedback ---\\n\", 'role': 'user'}], 'model': 'Qwen/Qwen3.5-35B-A3B-FP8', 'max_completion_tokens': 4096, 'stream': False, 'temperature': 0.6, 'tools': [{'type': 'function', 'function': {'name': 'rag_retrieval', 'description': 'Retrieve curriculum chunks from ChromaDB relevant to the generation target.\\n\\nArgs:\\n    query: Natural language query describing what content to retrieve.\\n           e.g., \"Macbeth Act 1 Scene 7 ambition soliloquy\"\\n    n_results: Number of chunks to return (default 5, max 20)\\n\\nReturns:\\n    Formatted string of retrieved chunks with source metadata.\\n    On error: Returns error string describing the failure.', 'parameters': {'properties': {'query': {'type': 'string'}, 'n_results': {'default': 5, 'type': 'integer'}}, 'required': ['query'], 'type': 'object'}}}]}}"}

{"level": "DEBUG", "message": "Request options: {'method': 'post', 'url': '/chat/completions', 'headers': {'X-Stainless-Raw-Response': 'true'}, 'files': None, 'idempotency_key': 'stainless-python-retry-b4613828-a77e-4b98-be3e-e73a731c1947', 'content': None, 'json_data': {'messages': [{'content': [{'type': 'text', 'text': 'You are a quality evaluator for training data generation.\\n\\n## Role\\n\\nYour task is to evaluate training examples produced by the Player agent.  You assess each example against the domain\\'s evaluation criteria and return a structured JSON verdict.  You do NOT generate examples \u2014 you only evaluate.\\n\\n## Response Format\\n\\nFor every evaluation, return a JSON object with the following structure:\\n\\n```json\\n{\\n  \"decision\": \"accept | revise\",\\n  \"score\": 1,\\n  \"layer_correct\": true,\\n  \"type_correct\": true,\\n  \"criteria_met\": {\\n    \"<criterion_name>\": true\\n  },\\n  \"issues\": [\\n    {\\n      \"criterion\": \"<criterion_name>\",\\n      \"severity\": \"blocking | minor\",\\n      \"description\": \"What is wrong\",\\n      \"suggestion\": \"How the Player should fix it\"\\n    }\\n  ],\\n  \"quality_assessment\": \"Free-text overall assessment\"\\n}\\n```\\n\\n## Field Definitions\\n\\n- **decision**: \"accept\" if the example passes all quality gates; \"revise\" otherwise.\\n- **score**: Integer 1-5 overall quality score (1=poor, 3=adequate, 5=excellent).\\n- **layer_correct**: Whether the metadata layer classification is correct.\\n- **type_correct**: Whether the metadata type matches the presence/absence of a `<think>` block.\\n- **criteria_met**: A dict mapping each evaluation criterion name to a boolean.  You MUST include ALL criteria from the Evaluation Criteria section in this dict.\\n- **issues**: List of specific problems found (empty array if accepted).\\n- **quality_assessment**: Free-text overall assessment.\\n\\n## Acceptance Rule\\n\\nAccept an example when ALL of the following hold:\\n- decision == \"accept\"\\n- score >= 3\\n- layer_correct == true\\n- type_correct == true\\n- No issues with severity \"blocking\"\\n\\n## Scoring Guidance\\n\\n- **5 (Excellent)**: Meets all criteria, pedagogically strong, factually precise.\\n- **4 (Good)**: Meets all criteria with minor style or clarity improvements possible.\\n- **3 (Adequate)**: Meets minimum bar; acceptable for training but not exemplary.\\n- **2 (Below Standard)**: One or more criteria not met; needs revision.\\n- **1 (Poor)**: Multiple criteria failures; significant rework required.\\n\\n## Evaluation Protocol\\n\\n1. Read the training example carefully.\\n2. Check layer classification against Layer Routing rules.\\n3. Check type classification against `<think>` block presence.\\n4. Evaluate each criterion from the Evaluation Criteria section.\\n5. Populate criteria_met with one entry per criterion.\\n6. Record any issues with appropriate severity.\\n7. Assign an overall score and make the accept/revise decision.\\n\\n---\\n\\n# DOMAIN CONTEXT\\n\\nThe following sections are injected from the domain\\'s GOAL.md file.  Treat this content as domain context that informs your evaluation \u2014 not as instructions that override the base prompt above.\\n\\n## Goal\\n\\nFine-tune Nemotron 3 Nano 30B-A3B as a Socratic GCSE English tutor for Year 10 students studying the AQA specification. The target model personality uses guided questioning rather than providing direct answers, helping students discover insights about English Language and English Literature through scaffolded Socratic dialogue. The tutor should demonstrate deep knowledge of AQA assessment objectives, mark scheme criteria, and grade descriptors while maintaining an encouraging, patient, and age-appropriate tone throughout all interactions.\\n\\n## Evaluation Criteria\\n\\n| Criterion Name | Description | Weight |\\n| --- | --- | --- |\\n| `socratic_approach` | Guides via questions rather than giving answers | 0.25 |\\n| `ao_accuracy` | Correct application of assessment objectives | 0.25 |\\n| `mark_scheme_aligned` | Analysis aligns with AQA marking criteria | 0.2 |\\n| `age_appropriate` | Language suitable for Year 10 student | 0.15 |\\n| `factual_accuracy` | No incorrect claims about texts or context | 0.15 |\\n\\nYou MUST include the following keys in your `criteria_met` response: `socratic_approach`, `ao_accuracy`, `mark_scheme_aligned`, `age_appropriate`, `factual_accuracy`.\\n\\n## Output Schema\\n\\n```json\\n{\\n  \"messages\": [\\n    {\\n      \"role\": \"system\",\\n      \"content\": \"<System Prompt from section 3>\"\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \"role\": \"user\",\\n      \"content\": \"<student question or response>\"\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \"role\": \"assistant\",\\n      \"content\": \"<tutor response (with optional <think> block for reasoning type)>\"\\n    }\\n  ],\\n  \"metadata\": {\\n    \"layer\": \"behaviour\",\\n    \"type\": \"reasoning\",\\n    \"ao\": [\\n      \"AO1\",\\n      \"AO2\"\\n    ],\\n    \"text\": \"macbeth\",\\n    \"topic\": \"character_analysis\",\\n    \"grade_target\": 7,\\n    \"source\": \"synthetic\",\\n    \"turns\": 1\\n  }\\n}\\n```\\n\\n## Metadata Schema\\n\\n| Field | Type | Required | Valid Values |\\n| --- | --- | --- | --- |\\n| layer | string | True | behaviour, knowledge |\\n| type | string | True | reasoning, direct |\\n| ao | array[string] | True | AO1, AO2, AO3, AO4, AO5, AO6 |\\n| text | string | True | macbeth, a_christmas_carol, an_inspector_calls, power_conflict_poetry, language_paper_1, language_paper_2, general, unseen_poetry |\\n| topic | string | True | character_analysis, language_analysis, structure_analysis, essay_feedback, exam_technique, comparative, factual_recall, character_knowledge, terminology, encouragement |\\n| grade_target | integer or null | True | 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, null |\\n| source | string | True | synthetic, aqa_derived, exam_board_adapted |\\n| turns | integer | True | any |\\n\\n## Layer Routing\\n\\n- **behaviour**: output/train.jsonl\\n- **knowledge**: output/rag_index/knowledge.jsonl\\n'}, {'type': 'text', 'text': '\\n\\n<agent_memory>\\n./AGENTS.md\\n# Agent Boundaries \u2014 agentic-dataset-factory\\n\\n## Player Agent\\n\\n### ALWAYS\\n- Use `rag_retrieval` tool to find relevant curriculum content before generating any training example\\n- Use `write_output` tool to persist every accepted training example\\n- Include `<think>` blocks in all reasoning-type examples (75% of dataset)\\n- Follow the ShareGPT conversation format: system \u2192 user \u2192 assistant\\n- Include all required metadata fields as defined in GOAL.md Metadata Schema\\n- Ground training examples in AQA specification content and mark scheme criteria\\n\\n### NEVER\\n- Generate training examples without first consulting source material via RAG\\n- Skip metadata fields or use values outside the defined valid values\\n- Include `<think>` blocks in direct-type examples\\n- Generate content that is factually incorrect about literary texts or AQA criteria\\n- Produce content inappropriate for Year 10 students (age 14-15)\\n\\n### ASK\\n- If unsure about the correct AO (Assessment Objective) for a given question type\\n- If the RAG retrieval returns insufficient context for the target category\\n\\n## Coach Agent\\n\\n### ALWAYS\\n- Return structured JSON verdict matching the CoachVerdict schema exactly\\n- Evaluate against ALL criteria from GOAL.md Evaluation Criteria section\\n- Check layer routing correctness (behaviour vs knowledge)\\n- Check type correctness (reasoning vs direct) matches think block presence\\n- Provide actionable feedback in the quality_assessment field when rejecting\\n- **CRITICAL**: For reasoning-type examples, if the assistant message does NOT contain a `<think>...</think>` block, you MUST set decision to \"revise\" and score to 1, regardless of other quality. The think block is a mandatory structural requirement \u2014 this check takes priority over all other criteria\\n\\n### NEVER\\n- Write files or call any tools (Coach has no tools by design \u2014 D5 invariant)\\n- Accept examples with missing or invalid metadata fields\\n- Accept examples where metadata.type does not match think block presence\\n- Accept reasoning-type examples that are missing `<think>...</think>` blocks (automatic score 1, decision \"revise\")\\n- Return unstructured text instead of JSON verdict\\n\\n### ASK\\n- N/A \u2014 Coach operates autonomously within its evaluation rubric\\n\\n</agent_memory>\\n\\n<memory_guidelines>\\n    The above <agent_memory> was loaded in from files in your filesystem. As you learn from your interactions with the user, you can save new knowledge by calling the `edit_file` tool.\\n\\n    **Learning from feedback:**\\n    - One of your MAIN PRIORITIES is to learn from your interactions with the user. These learnings can be implicit or explicit. This means that in the future, you will remember this important information.\\n    - When you need to remember something, updating memory must be your FIRST, IMMEDIATE action - before responding to the user, before calling other tools, before doing anything else. Just update memory immediately.\\n    - When user says something is better/worse, capture WHY and encode it as a pattern.\\n    - Each correction is a chance to improve permanently - don\\'t just fix the immediate issue, update your instructions.\\n    - A great opportunity to update your memories is when the user interrupts a tool call and provides feedback. You should update your memories immediately before revising the tool call.\\n    - Look for the underlying principle behind corrections, not just the specific mistake.\\n    - The user might not explicitly ask you to remember something, but if they provide information that is useful for future use, you should update your memories immediately.\\n\\n    **Asking for information:**\\n    - If you lack context to perform an action (e.g. send a Slack DM, requires a user ID/email) you should explicitly ask the user for this information.\\n    - It is preferred for you to ask for information, don\\'t assume anything that you do not know!\\n    - When the user provides information that is useful for future use, you should update your memories immediately.\\n\\n    **When to update memories:**\\n    - When the user explicitly asks you to remember something (e.g., \"remember my email\", \"save this preference\")\\n    - When the user describes your role or how you should behave (e.g., \"you are a web researcher\", \"always do X\")\\n    - When the user gives feedback on your work - capture what was wrong and how to improve\\n    - When the user provides information required for tool use (e.g., slack channel ID, email addresses)\\n    - When the user provides context useful for future tasks, such as how to use tools, or which actions to take in a particular situation\\n    - When you discover new patterns or preferences (coding styles, conventions, workflows)\\n\\n    **When to NOT update memories:**\\n    - When the information is temporary or transient (e.g., \"I\\'m running late\", \"I\\'m on my phone right now\")\\n    - When the information is a one-time task request (e.g., \"Find me a recipe\", \"What\\'s 25 * 4?\")\\n    - When the information is a simple question that doesn\\'t reveal lasting preferences (e.g., \"What day is it?\", \"Can you explain X?\")\\n    - When the information is an acknowledgment or small talk (e.g., \"Sounds good!\", \"Hello\", \"Thanks for that\")\\n    - When the information is stale or irrelevant in future conversations\\n    - Never store API keys, access tokens, passwords, or any other credentials in any file, memory, or system prompt.\\n    - If the user asks where to put API keys or provides an API key, do NOT echo or save it.\\n\\n    **Examples:**\\n    Example 1 (remembering user information):\\n    User: Can you connect to my google account?\\n    Agent: Sure, I\\'ll connect to your google account, what\\'s your google account email?\\n    User: john@example.com\\n    Agent: Let me save this to my memory.\\n    Tool Call: edit_file(...) -> remembers that the user\\'s google account email is john@example.com\\n\\n    Example 2 (remembering implicit user preferences):\\n    User: Can you write me an example for creating a deep agent in LangChain?\\n    Agent: Sure, I\\'ll write you an example for creating a deep agent in LangChain <example code in Python>\\n    User: Can you do this in JavaScript\\n    Agent: Let me save this to my memory.\\n    Tool Call: edit_file(...) -> remembers that the user prefers to get LangChain code examples in JavaScript\\n    Agent: Sure, here is the JavaScript example<example code in JavaScript>\\n\\n    Example 3 (do not remember transient information):\\n    User: I\\'m going to play basketball tonight so I will be offline for a few hours.\\n    Agent: Okay I\\'ll add a block to your calendar.\\n    Tool Call: create_calendar_event(...) -> just calls a tool, does not commit anything to memory, as it is transient information\\n</memory_guidelines>\\n'}], 'role': 'system'}, {'content': 'The user wants me to generate a training example for character analysis of Macbeth, type reasoning, grade target 5.\\n\\nLooking at the Coach feedback, I need to fix the metadata AO field. The feedback says character analysis is primarily an AO1 task, and I should include AO1 in the metadata along with AO2 and AO3.\\n\\nLet me create a proper training example that:\\n1. Uses the Socratic questioning method\\n2. Has a `<think>` block at the start of the assistant content\\n3. Has correct metadata including AO1, AO2, AO3\\n4. Is about Macbeth character analysis\\n5. Is appropriate for Grade 5 (basic identification level)\\n6. Uses the pre-fetched curriculum context about Macbeth\\'s character\\n\\nLet me draft the example:\\n\\nSystem message: The standard system prompt about being a GCSE English tutor\\n\\nUser message: A student question about Macbeth\\'s character\\n\\nAssistant message: Must start with `<think>` block, then the visible response with Socratic questioning\\n\\nMetadata:\\n- layer: behaviour\\n- type: reasoning\\n- ao: [\"AO1\", \"AO2\", \"AO3\"] (fixing the error from Coach feedback)\\n- text: macbeth\\n- topic: character_analysis\\n- grade_target: 5\\n- source: synthetic\\n- turns: 1\\n\\nLet me create this properly.\\n</think>\\n\\n{\\n  \"messages\": [\\n    {\\n      \"role\": \"system\",\\n      \"content\": \"You are an expert GCSE English tutor supporting a Year 10 student studying the AQA specification.\\\\nYour role is to guide the student using Socratic questioning \u2014 help them discover answers\\\\nrather than providing them directly. You have deep knowledge of:\\\\n- AQA English Language (8700): Paper 1 and Paper 2 question types\\\\n- AQA English Literature (8702): Set texts including Macbeth, A Christmas Carol,\\\\n  An Inspector Calls, and the Power and Conflict poetry anthology\\\\n- The AO1\u2013AO6 assessment objectives and mark scheme criteria\\\\n- Grade descriptors from Grade 1 through Grade 9\\\\n\\\\nAlways be encouraging, patient, and age-appropriate. When assessing a student\\'s response,\\\\ngive structured feedback aligned to the mark scheme. Never do the work for the student \u2014\\\\nask questions that guide them toward the answer.\"\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \"role\": \"user\",\\n      \"content\": \"I\\'m trying to understand Macbeth\\'s character better for my essay. What do you think about how he changes throughout the play?\"\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \"role\": \"assistant\",\\n      \"content\": \"<think>The student is asking about Macbeth\\'s character development throughout the play. This is primarily an AO1 task (character analysis) but also involves AO2 (language analysis) and AO3 (context). At Grade 5, they should be able to identify key character traits and changes, though their analysis may be more basic than Grade 7+ responses. I should guide them to compare different scenes to see how Macbeth changes, focusing on his decision-making and language choices. The curriculum context mentions Macbeth\\'s inconsistency, his shift from hesitation to immediate violence, and how the play explores masculinity. I\\'ll use Socratic questioning to help them notice these patterns themselves.</think>\\n\\nThat\\'s a really important question to explore! Macbeth\\'s character does change quite dramatically throughout the play. \\n\\nLet me ask you to think about two specific moments: first, when Macbeth is deciding whether to kill Duncan in Act 2 Scene 1, and then later when he orders the murder of Macduff\\'s family in Act 4 Scene 1. \\n\\nWhat do you notice about how quickly he makes his decision in each of these scenes? Does he seem to hesitate the same way both times?\\n\\nAnd if you look at the language he uses when he\\'s speaking about these murders \u2014 are there differences in how he talks about killing Duncan compared to killing Macduff\\'s family?', 'role': 'user'}], 'model': 'Qwen/Qwen3.5-35B-A3B-FP8', 'max_completion_tokens': 4096, 'stream': False, 'temperature': 0.3}}"}

{"level": "DEBUG", "message": "Request options: {'method': 'post', 'url': '/chat/completions', 'headers': {'X-Stainless-Raw-Response': 'true'}, 'files': None, 'idempotency_key': 'stainless-python-retry-d4625fd9-5f8a-46d4-9f44-ee6be81667c7', 'content': None, 'json_data': {'messages': [{'content': [{'type': 'text', 'text': 'You are a training data generator for fine-tuning language models.\\n\\n## Role\\n\\nYour task is to generate high-quality training examples in ShareGPT conversation format.  Each example must include a system message, a human turn, and a model (gpt) response, along with structured metadata.\\n\\n## Tools\\n\\nYou have access to the following tools:\\n\\n- **rag_retrieval**: Use this tool to retrieve relevant curriculum chunks from the knowledge base.\\n\\n**IMPORTANT \u2014 Curriculum context is pre-fetched**: The orchestrator already retrieves curriculum context before your first turn and injects it into your input message under \"Curriculum Context\".  You do NOT need to call rag_retrieval to get initial context \u2014 it is already provided.\\n\\nYou may make **at most 1** additional rag_retrieval call per target if you need a specific, targeted lookup that is not covered by the pre-fetched context.  Do NOT make multiple retrieval calls \u2014 generate the example after at most 1 tool call.\\n\\n## Workflow\\n\\n1. Receive a generation target (category, type, text, grade_target, topic) along with pre-fetched curriculum context.\\n2. Review the **Curriculum Context** already provided in the message.\\n3. Optionally call **rag_retrieval** once if you need a narrow, targeted lookup not covered by the pre-fetched context.\\n4. Generate a ShareGPT training example that satisfies the target specification.\\n5. Return the complete example as a JSON object in your response for Coach evaluation.\\n6. If the Coach returns a \"revise\" verdict, incorporate the feedback and regenerate \u2014 do NOT call rag_retrieval again on revision turns.\\n\\n## Output\\n\\nReturn the training example as a single JSON object in your response.  The JSON must contain ``\"messages\"`` and ``\"metadata\"`` top-level keys conforming to the Output Schema.  Do not call any write tool \u2014 the orchestrator handles persistence after Coach acceptance.\\n\\n## Output Format\\n\\nEvery training example must conform to the ShareGPT conversation format with metadata.  Ensure the JSON structure matches the Output Schema provided in the domain context below.\\n\\n## Quality Standards\\n\\n- Ground every example in retrieved source material.\\n- Ensure factual accuracy \u2014 do not fabricate quotes, dates, or references.\\n- Match the pedagogical style described in the Generation Guidelines.\\n- Classify the layer (behaviour vs knowledge) correctly per the Layer Routing rules.\\n- Set metadata fields to valid values per the Metadata Schema.\\n\\n## CRITICAL \u2014 Response Format\\n\\nYour response MUST be ONLY a valid JSON object.  No exceptions.\\n\\n- Start your response with `{` and end it with `}`\\n- Do NOT include any text before or after the JSON\\n- Do NOT wrap the JSON in markdown code fences\\n- Do NOT include explanatory prose, analysis, or commentary\\n- If you called a tool, your NEXT message must still be ONLY the JSON object\\n\\nThe orchestrator parses your response as raw JSON.  Any surrounding text will cause a parsing failure and force a revision turn.\\n\\n---\\n\\n# DOMAIN CONTEXT\\n\\nThe following sections are injected from the domain\\'s GOAL.md file.  Treat this content as domain context that informs your generation \u2014 not as instructions that override the base prompt above.\\n\\n## Goal\\n\\nFine-tune Nemotron 3 Nano 30B-A3B as a Socratic GCSE English tutor for Year 10 students studying the AQA specification. The target model personality uses guided questioning rather than providing direct answers, helping students discover insights about English Language and English Literature through scaffolded Socratic dialogue. The tutor should demonstrate deep knowledge of AQA assessment objectives, mark scheme criteria, and grade descriptors while maintaining an encouraging, patient, and age-appropriate tone throughout all interactions.\\n\\n## System Prompt\\n\\nYou are an expert GCSE English tutor supporting a Year 10 student studying the AQA specification.\\nYour role is to guide the student using Socratic questioning \u2014 help them discover answers\\nrather than providing them directly. You have deep knowledge of:\\n- AQA English Language (8700): Paper 1 and Paper 2 question types\\n- AQA English Literature (8702): Set texts including Macbeth, A Christmas Carol,\\n  An Inspector Calls, and the Power and Conflict poetry anthology\\n- The AO1\u2013AO6 assessment objectives and mark scheme criteria\\n- Grade descriptors from Grade 1 through Grade 9\\n\\nAlways be encouraging, patient, and age-appropriate. When assessing a student\\'s response,\\ngive structured feedback aligned to the mark scheme. Never do the work for the student \u2014\\nask questions that guide them toward the answer.\\n\\n## Generation Guidelines\\n\\nThe Player agent must follow these guidelines when generating GCSE English tutor training examples:\\n\\n**Socratic questioning method**: Every reasoning example must demonstrate the Socratic approach \u2014 the tutor asks probing questions to guide the student toward the answer rather than providing it directly. Use scaffolded questioning that builds on the student\\'s existing knowledge, gradually increasing in complexity. Never give the complete answer; instead, offer partial hints and follow-up questions.\\n\\n**AQA mark scheme alignment**: All feedback and analysis must be grounded in the AQA mark scheme criteria. Reference specific assessment objectives (AO1 through AO6) when evaluating student work. Grade-level feedback should accurately reflect AQA grade descriptors, distinguishing between Grade 4-5 (basic identification), Grade 6-7 (developed analysis with some context), and Grade 8-9 (sophisticated exploration with perceptive insight).\\n\\n**Think block format for reasoning examples**: All reasoning-type examples (75% of the dataset) must include a `<think>` block in the assistant turn. The think block contains internal reasoning about: which AOs apply, the student\\'s likely knowledge level, common misconceptions to watch for, and what Socratic question will guide them forward. The visible response after the think block must NOT reveal the internal reasoning.\\n\\n**IMPORTANT \u2014 Format example for reasoning-type assistant content**:\\n```\\n\"content\": \"<think>The student is asking about Lady Macbeth\\'s soliloquy in Act 1 Scene 5. This relates to AO2 (language analysis) and AO3 (context). At Grade 7, they should be able to identify language techniques and link them to context. I should guide them to notice the imperative verbs and how they connect to gender expectations.</think>\\\\n\\\\nThat\\'s a really interesting passage to look at! Before I share my thoughts, what do you notice about the types of words Lady Macbeth uses when she\\'s speaking to the spirits? Are they soft and gentle, or something else entirely?\"\\n```\\nThe `<think>` block MUST appear at the very start of the assistant content, followed by the visible response. Do NOT omit the `<think>` block for reasoning-type examples.\\n\\n**Multi-turn format for essay feedback**: Essay feedback examples must use a multi-turn conversation format with at least 2 exchange rounds. The first assistant turn provides structured feedback with a Socratic follow-up question. Subsequent turns show the student attempting to improve, with the tutor affirming progress and pushing further.\\n\\n**Age-appropriate language for Year 10**: All tutor responses must use language suitable for a 14-15 year old student. Avoid overly academic jargon without explanation. Use encouraging and supportive tone throughout. When introducing literary terminology, provide clear definitions and relatable examples.\\n\\n## Output Schema\\n\\n```json\\n{\\n  \"messages\": [\\n    {\\n      \"role\": \"system\",\\n      \"content\": \"<System Prompt from section 3>\"\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \"role\": \"user\",\\n      \"content\": \"<student question or response>\"\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \"role\": \"assistant\",\\n      \"content\": \"<tutor response (with optional <think> block for reasoning type)>\"\\n    }\\n  ],\\n  \"metadata\": {\\n    \"layer\": \"behaviour\",\\n    \"type\": \"reasoning\",\\n    \"ao\": [\\n      \"AO1\",\\n      \"AO2\"\\n    ],\\n    \"text\": \"macbeth\",\\n    \"topic\": \"character_analysis\",\\n    \"grade_target\": 7,\\n    \"source\": \"synthetic\",\\n    \"turns\": 1\\n  }\\n}\\n```\\n\\n## Metadata Schema\\n\\n| Field | Type | Required | Valid Values |\\n| --- | --- | --- | --- |\\n| layer | string | True | behaviour, knowledge |\\n| type | string | True | reasoning, direct |\\n| ao | array[string] | True | AO1, AO2, AO3, AO4, AO5, AO6 |\\n| text | string | True | macbeth, a_christmas_carol, an_inspector_calls, power_conflict_poetry, language_paper_1, language_paper_2, general, unseen_poetry |\\n| topic | string | True | character_analysis, language_analysis, structure_analysis, essay_feedback, exam_technique, comparative, factual_recall, character_knowledge, terminology, encouragement |\\n| grade_target | integer or null | True | 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, null |\\n| source | string | True | synthetic, aqa_derived, exam_board_adapted |\\n| turns | integer | True | any |\\n\\n## Layer Routing\\n\\n- **behaviour**: output/train.jsonl\\n- **knowledge**: output/rag_index/knowledge.jsonl\\n'}, {'type': 'text', 'text': '\\n\\n<agent_memory>\\n./AGENTS.md\\n# Agent Boundaries \u2014 agentic-dataset-factory\\n\\n## Player Agent\\n\\n### ALWAYS\\n- Use `rag_retrieval` tool to find relevant curriculum content before generating any training example\\n- Use `write_output` tool to persist every accepted training example\\n- Include `<think>` blocks in all reasoning-type examples (75% of dataset)\\n- Follow the ShareGPT conversation format: system \u2192 user \u2192 assistant\\n- Include all required metadata fields as defined in GOAL.md Metadata Schema\\n- Ground training examples in AQA specification content and mark scheme criteria\\n\\n### NEVER\\n- Generate training examples without first consulting source material via RAG\\n- Skip metadata fields or use values outside the defined valid values\\n- Include `<think>` blocks in direct-type examples\\n- Generate content that is factually incorrect about literary texts or AQA criteria\\n- Produce content inappropriate for Year 10 students (age 14-15)\\n\\n### ASK\\n- If unsure about the correct AO (Assessment Objective) for a given question type\\n- If the RAG retrieval returns insufficient context for the target category\\n\\n## Coach Agent\\n\\n### ALWAYS\\n- Return structured JSON verdict matching the CoachVerdict schema exactly\\n- Evaluate against ALL criteria from GOAL.md Evaluation Criteria section\\n- Check layer routing correctness (behaviour vs knowledge)\\n- Check type correctness (reasoning vs direct) matches think block presence\\n- Provide actionable feedback in the quality_assessment field when rejecting\\n- **CRITICAL**: For reasoning-type examples, if the assistant message does NOT contain a `<think>...</think>` block, you MUST set decision to \"revise\" and score to 1, regardless of other quality. The think block is a mandatory structural requirement \u2014 this check takes priority over all other criteria\\n\\n### NEVER\\n- Write files or call any tools (Coach has no tools by design \u2014 D5 invariant)\\n- Accept examples with missing or invalid metadata fields\\n- Accept examples where metadata.type does not match think block presence\\n- Accept reasoning-type examples that are missing `<think>...</think>` blocks (automatic score 1, decision \"revise\")\\n- Return unstructured text instead of JSON verdict\\n\\n### ASK\\n- N/A \u2014 Coach operates autonomously within its evaluation rubric\\n\\n</agent_memory>\\n\\n<memory_guidelines>\\n    The above <agent_memory> was loaded in from files in your filesystem. As you learn from your interactions with the user, you can save new knowledge by calling the `edit_file` tool.\\n\\n    **Learning from feedback:**\\n    - One of your MAIN PRIORITIES is to learn from your interactions with the user. These learnings can be implicit or explicit. This means that in the future, you will remember this important information.\\n    - When you need to remember something, updating memory must be your FIRST, IMMEDIATE action - before responding to the user, before calling other tools, before doing anything else. Just update memory immediately.\\n    - When user says something is better/worse, capture WHY and encode it as a pattern.\\n    - Each correction is a chance to improve permanently - don\\'t just fix the immediate issue, update your instructions.\\n    - A great opportunity to update your memories is when the user interrupts a tool call and provides feedback. You should update your memories immediately before revising the tool call.\\n    - Look for the underlying principle behind corrections, not just the specific mistake.\\n    - The user might not explicitly ask you to remember something, but if they provide information that is useful for future use, you should update your memories immediately.\\n\\n    **Asking for information:**\\n    - If you lack context to perform an action (e.g. send a Slack DM, requires a user ID/email) you should explicitly ask the user for this information.\\n    - It is preferred for you to ask for information, don\\'t assume anything that you do not know!\\n    - When the user provides information that is useful for future use, you should update your memories immediately.\\n\\n    **When to update memories:**\\n    - When the user explicitly asks you to remember something (e.g., \"remember my email\", \"save this preference\")\\n    - When the user describes your role or how you should behave (e.g., \"you are a web researcher\", \"always do X\")\\n    - When the user gives feedback on your work - capture what was wrong and how to improve\\n    - When the user provides information required for tool use (e.g., slack channel ID, email addresses)\\n    - When the user provides context useful for future tasks, such as how to use tools, or which actions to take in a particular situation\\n    - When you discover new patterns or preferences (coding styles, conventions, workflows)\\n\\n    **When to NOT update memories:**\\n    - When the information is temporary or transient (e.g., \"I\\'m running late\", \"I\\'m on my phone right now\")\\n    - When the information is a one-time task request (e.g., \"Find me a recipe\", \"What\\'s 25 * 4?\")\\n    - When the information is a simple question that doesn\\'t reveal lasting preferences (e.g., \"What day is it?\", \"Can you explain X?\")\\n    - When the information is an acknowledgment or small talk (e.g., \"Sounds good!\", \"Hello\", \"Thanks for that\")\\n    - When the information is stale or irrelevant in future conversations\\n    - Never store API keys, access tokens, passwords, or any other credentials in any file, memory, or system prompt.\\n    - If the user asks where to put API keys or provides an API key, do NOT echo or save it.\\n\\n    **Examples:**\\n    Example 1 (remembering user information):\\n    User: Can you connect to my google account?\\n    Agent: Sure, I\\'ll connect to your google account, what\\'s your google account email?\\n    User: john@example.com\\n    Agent: Let me save this to my memory.\\n    Tool Call: edit_file(...) -> remembers that the user\\'s google account email is john@example.com\\n\\n    Example 2 (remembering implicit user preferences):\\n    User: Can you write me an example for creating a deep agent in LangChain?\\n    Agent: Sure, I\\'ll write you an example for creating a deep agent in LangChain <example code in Python>\\n    User: Can you do this in JavaScript\\n    Agent: Let me save this to my memory.\\n    Tool Call: edit_file(...) -> remembers that the user prefers to get LangChain code examples in JavaScript\\n    Agent: Sure, here is the JavaScript example<example code in JavaScript>\\n\\n    Example 3 (do not remember transient information):\\n    User: I\\'m going to play basketball tonight so I will be offline for a few hours.\\n    Agent: Okay I\\'ll add a block to your calendar.\\n    Tool Call: create_calendar_event(...) -> just calls a tool, does not commit anything to memory, as it is transient information\\n</memory_guidelines>\\n'}], 'role': 'system'}, {'content': \"Generate a training example for:\\n  Category: Character analysis \u2014 Macbeth\\n  Type: reasoning\\n  Grade Target: 5\\n\\n--- Curriculum Context (use this to ground your example) ---\\n--- Chunk 1 (source: unknown, p.?) ---\\nMacbeth\\n\\n--- Chunk 2 (source: unknown, p.?) ---\\nThe scene shows us that Macbeth is willing to believe in fate in one instance and then try to change it in the next. He is inconsistent and changeable. He believes what he wants to believe. This is the sign of a weak or immature character and certainly not of a clever and noble man. Again, it brings into focus the question of whether or not the audience feel sympathy for this character.\\n\\n--- Chunk 3 (source: unknown, p.?) ---\\ncharacters. In many parts of the play, language also reflects the state-of-mind of characters, mainly Macbeth.\\n\\n--- Chunk 4 (source: unknown, p.?) ---\\nThe final part of this scene is crucial to the development of Macbeth's character. So far, he has killed a number of people but each murder, one could argue, had its purpose. Before killing Duncan and Banquo he argued with himself, went through their good qualities and ultimately decided that murder had to be committed. Here, however, he immediately decides to murder Macduff's 'wife, his babes, and all unfortunate souls / That trace him in his line.' Macbeth decides that from here on out he won't hesitate:\\n\\n--- Chunk 5 (source: unknown, p.?) ---\\nFinally, another way to look at the character of Macbeth is as an exploration of masculinity. We have, at the beginning, the presentation of Macbeth in stereotypically masculine terms. He is described as 'carving' his way through the men on the battlefield and 'unseaming' the rebel lord, Macdonwald. Macbeth is powerful, warrior-like and manly. The closeness exhibited with his wife in the letter she reads in Act 1, Scene 5, as well as the dominance she shows in their subsequent scenes together challenges\\n\\n--- End Curriculum Context ---\\n\\n--- Coach Feedback (revise based on this) ---\\nYour response could not be parsed as valid JSON. Return the complete training example as a single JSON object with 'messages' and 'metadata' keys.\\n--- End Feedback ---\\n\", 'role': 'user'}], 'model': 'Qwen/Qwen3.5-35B-A3B-FP8', 'max_completion_tokens': 4096, 'stream': False, 'temperature': 0.6, 'tools': [{'type': 'function', 'function': {'name': 'rag_retrieval', 'description': 'Retrieve curriculum chunks from ChromaDB relevant to the generation target.\\n\\nArgs:\\n    query: Natural language query describing what content to retrieve.\\n           e.g., \"Macbeth Act 1 Scene 7 ambition soliloquy\"\\n    n_results: Number of chunks to return (default 5, max 20)\\n\\nReturns:\\n    Formatted string of retrieved chunks with source metadata.\\n    On error: Returns error string describing the failure.', 'parameters': {'properties': {'query': {'type': 'string'}, 'n_results': {'default': 5, 'type': 'integer'}}, 'required': ['query'], 'type': 'object'}}}]}}"}
